I spend my days patching the leaking coolant veins of the Capital Spire, knowing that when the pressure finally blows, the seat of the Emperor's power will freeze solid.
The nobility still call me a servant, but they beg for my mercy when the ancient waste-conduits of their floating estates begin to vomit raw sludge into their gilded ballrooms.
I’ve learned to navigate the lightless subterranean labyrinths of the Outer Rim, where the rusted pipes hum with the dying echoes of a civilization that forgot how to maintain its own arteries.
My toolkit is a collection of scavenged relics from a dozen fallen star-systems, the only things capable of sealing a breach in a plasma-main before it vaporizes an entire residential sector.
As the bureaucratic machinery of the empire grinds to a halt, I am the only one left who knows which rusted valve to turn to keep the oxygen flowing to the lower wards.
I don't care who owns the company after the merger, but if these executive restrooms keep overflowing, the whole boardroom is going to smell like a sewer.
I’m just here to fix the burst pipe in the server room so the lawyers can keep pushing through the final acquisition paperwork.
These high-powered CEOs are shouting about synergies and stock options, but they can't even figure out how to stop a leak in the breakroom sink.
I’ve spent all morning winding a snake through the plumbing of the penthouse suite while the merger negotiations reach a fever pitch upstairs.
The tension in this building is thick enough to clog a drain, and I'm the only one here actually dealing with the filth.
I have spent all morning digging a deeper trench to redirect the muddy runoff away from the Chief's sleeping hut before the rival clan arrives.
I am trying to explain to the tribal elders that the leak in their ceremonial water basin is caused by a hairline crack in the stone, not a curse from the mountain spirits.
I used a mixture of pine resin and river clay to seal the bamboo pipes that bring fresh spring water into the village center.
I refuse to let the war party leave for the valley until I have finished clearing the blockage of mammoth hair and silt from the communal drainage pit.
I am convinced that if we simply reroute the stream using these hollowed logs, we can starve the opposing tribe of their water supply without fighting a single battle.
I spend my shifts diving into the neon-lit capillaries of the lower wards to purge the crystalline sludge that threatens to choke the city's circulation.
When the central consciousness suffers a sensory hemorrhage, I am the one who descends into the humming depths to solder the leaking data-conduits.
I can tell by the vibration in my wrench exactly which subterranean valve is failing to regulate the flow of liquid coolant to the city's overheating processors.
The sentient architecture often groans in a frequency only I understand, signaling a rupture in the nutrient-slurry lines that feed the vertical gardens.
I navigate the shifting geometry of the maintenance tunnels, patching the iridescent leaks of raw energy that bleed from the city's synthetic arteries.
I don't care if this is the High Court of the Afterlife; there is a leak in the ethereal pipes beneath the judge's bench that is flooding the purgatorial records.
My witness testimony is simple: I was just trying to clear a clog of solidified sins from the drain when the demon bailiff tried to tackle me.
You can keep your divine mandates, but until someone pays me for the emergency overtime spent fixing the leak in the Fountain of Souls, I'm not leaving this courtroom.
I've worked on some nasty jobs in the mortal realm, but scrubbing the spectral slime out of these celestial conduits is a whole different level of filth.
I am formally requesting a recess because the plumbing in the defendant's holding cell has burst, and the resulting flood of ectoplasm is a serious health hazard.
I can hear the hull groaning under the pressure, but it's the rhythmic drip of salt water through a hairline fracture in the bulkhead that tells me exactly where the seal failed.
These geothermal vents provide a hell of a lot of heat, but they've warped the main coolant lines into shapes that would make a normal technician quit on the spot.
The bioluminescent algae have clogged the filtration intake, turning the recycled water into a glowing, viscous sludge that refuses to flow.
I don't care if the station is officially derelict; someone still has to patch the oxidation holes in the waste disposal system before we all drown in our own filth.
Navigating these flooded corridors is a nightmare, but I've found that a heavy-duty pipe wrench is the only tool that speaks the same language as this rusted, dying machinery.
I find the installation of leaking pipes in the center of the gallery quite amateurish, as the drip rate is inconsistent and the sealant is poorly applied.
These minimalist white walls are far too pristine, making me worry about the inevitable water damage if one of these conceptual fountains fails.
I spent an hour staring at the sculpture of the rusted valve, wondering why the artist chose to ignore the obvious corrosion on the threading.
The curator's explanation of "fluidity as a metaphor for grief" doesn't change the fact that there is a significant blockage in the building's main riser.
While the critics praise the avant-garde use of copper tubing, I can't help but notice that the joints aren't soldered to code.
I spend my days dredging the city's clogged gutters, hoping the foul sludge doesn't carry the same rot that has claimed half the neighborhood.
My tools are useless against a sickness of the blood, yet I am tasked with sealing the lead pipes of the wealthy to keep the miasma from seeping into their cellars.
I avoid the plague doctors in their beaked masks, for I know that no amount of aromatic herbs can hide the stench of a burst sewer line in the heat of July.
Every time I descend into the damp tunnels to clear a blockage, I fear that the rats scurrying across my boots are the true heralds of the Great Mortality.
I charge double for my services now, as the survivors are desperate to flush the filth of the dead from their homes before the wind shifts.
I had to patch a leak in the kitchen sink, but the water flowing out was actually a stream of raw binary code that stained the floorboards.
I reached into the wall to tighten a valve and found that the copper piping had phased into a translucent, humming geometry that didn't obey the laws of physics.
The client complained about a clog, but when I opened the drain, I saw a mirrored reflection of the entire neighborhood looping infinitely inside the pipe.
I tried to solder a joint in the basement, only to realize the metal was flickering in and out of existence like a dying lightbulb.
After I cleared the main line, the water started flowing backward in time, scrubbing the rust off the pipes until they looked brand new.
